revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[en:Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii]][[ja:ハワイ出雲大社]] [[File:USVA headstone emb-34.svg|thumb|[[Izumo-taishakyo]]]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Hawaii Izumo Taisha
ハワイ出雲大社 | image = File:IZUMO TAISHA-KYO MISSION 01.jpg | caption = | map_type = Hawaii | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|21.3156|N|-157.861|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = Location within Hawaii | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]], Hawaii [[Ubusunagami|Ubusuna]]-no-{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Kami|1=Shinto shrine}} | founded_by = | established = 1906 | date_destroyed = | location = 215 N Kukui St, Honolulu, HI 96817 | website = {{URL|https://www.facebook.com/Izumo-Taishakyo-Mission-of-Hawaii-162743883736181/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Taisha-zukuri|qid=Q387874|1=Taisha-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = Bishop Daiya Amano
Rev. Jun Miyasaka | location = {{ill|Honolulu|qid=Q18094|1=Honolulu}} }} The '''Izumo Taishakyo Mission''' is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in downtown [[:en:Honolulu, Hawaii|Honolulu]], {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|qid=Q782|lt=Hawaii|1=Hawaii}}. It is one of the few active [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} in the United States. The wooden A-frame structure was inspired by {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}'s classical Japanese shrine [[Izumo-taisha]]. It was designed by architect {{ill|Hego Fuchino|en|Hego Fuchino|qid=Q58471955|lt=Hego Fuchino|1=Hego Fuchino}} and built by master carpenter [[Ichisaburo Takata]]. The primary ''[[kami]]'' of this shrine is [[Ōkuninushi]] and Hawaii [[Ubusunagami|Ubusuna]]-no-{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Kami|1=Shinto shrine}}. Also enshrined are [[Okinawa Shrine]], {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, [[Futemma-gū|Futenma Shrine]], {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|qid=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}}, and {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu Shrine|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, and [[:en:Waianae, Hawaii|Waianae]] [[Ujigami]].Miyao, Richard T. Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii Centennial Anniversary Saga of a Church in Hawaii, 1906-2006. Hawaii Hochi, Ltd. Honolulu, Hawaii. 2006. p. 81. The shrine is the site of the annual New Year's Day ''{{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|qid=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}}'' as well as other events throughout the year. A replica of the [[Hiroshima Peace Bell]] is on view. The annual Hiroshima Commemoration and Peace Service is held at Hawaii Izumo Taisha to commemorate the {{ill|Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|en|Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|qid=Q488|lt=atomic bombing of Hiroshima|1=Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki}}.[http://thehawaiiherald.com/2015/09/peace-events-commemorate-70th-anniversary-of-the-end-of-world-war-ii/ "Peace Events Commemorate 70th Anniversary Of The End Of World War II"], ''[[The Hawaii Herald]]'', Honolulu, Retrieved on 31 January 2017. ==History== Hawaii Izumo Taisha was founded in 1906 when Rev. Katsuyoshi Miyao opened a temporary {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} on Aala Street near Aala Park on 26 September 1906.Maeda, Takakazu. Hawaii no Jinjashi. Taimeido. Tokyo. 1999. p. 153. A temporary shrine building was completed on 25 August 1907. A permanent [[honden|shrine building]] was completed in 1922.Maeda, Takakazu. Hawaii no Jinjashi. Taimeido. Tokyo. 1999. p. 161. By 1941, there were branches of Izumo Taishakyo operating in {{ill|Hilo, Hawaii|en|Hilo, Hawaii|qid=Q216258|lt=Hilo|1=Hilo, Hawaii}}, {{ill|Wailuku, Hawaii|en|Wailuku, Hawaii|qid=Q2087831|lt=Wailuku|1=Wailuku, Hawaii}}, {{ill|Waipahu, Hawaii|en|Waipahu, Hawaii|qid=Q637246|lt=Waipahu|1=Waipahu, Hawaii}}, {{ill|Pearl City, Hawaii|en|Pearl City, Hawaii|qid=Q572052|lt=Pearl City|1=Pearl City, Hawaii}}, [[:en:Honouliuli|Honouliuli]], Ewa Lower Camp, [[:en:Aiea, Hawaii|Aiea]], and [[:en:Kakaako, Hawaii|Kakaako]].Maeda, Takakazu. Hawaii no Jinjashi. Taimeido. Tokyo. 1999. p. 174. The shrine was closed on 7 December 1941 at the outset of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}. The shrine was illegally acquired by the {{ill|Honolulu County, Hawaii|en|Honolulu County, Hawaii|qid=Q487704|lt=City and County of Honolulu|1=Honolulu County, Hawaii}} in June 1942. After {{ill|Internment of Japanese Americans|en|Internment of Japanese Americans|qid=Q936604|lt=internment|1=Internment of Japanese Americans}} on the mainland, the priest and family returned to Hawaii in mid-December 1945. A temporary shrine was consecrated in a residence-like warehouse in McCully area of Honolulu and served as a {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} from 1946-1968.Miyao, Richard T. Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii Centennial Anniversary Saga of a Church in Hawaii, 1906-2006. Hawaii Hochi, Ltd. Honolulu, Hawaii. 2006. p. 87-88. The original shrine property was returned to the shrine organization in October 1961 as a result of lengthy legal and legislative appeals.Maeda, Takakazu. Hawaii no Jinjashi. Taimeido. Tokyo. 1999. p. 180. The shrine building was moved in 1963 to its present location to make way for {{ill|Federal Housing Administration|en|Federal Housing Administration|qid=Q474305|lt=Federal Housing Administration|1=Federal Housing Administration}} redevelopment. The shrine was restored from 1968-1969 at a cost of $170,000. Funds were donated entirely by the people of Hawaii. The shrine was rededicated on 22 December 1968.Miyao, Richard T. Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii Centennial Anniversary Saga of a Church in Hawaii, 1906-2006. Hawaii Hochi, Ltd. Honolulu, Hawaii. 2006. p. 92. ==See also== *{{ill|Japanese Peace Bell|en|Japanese Peace Bell|qid=Q2067390|lt=Japanese Peace Bell|1=Japanese Peace Bell}} *{{ill|Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park|en|Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park|qid=Q1207208|lt=Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park|1=Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park}} *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines in the United States|en|List of Shinto shrines in the United States|qid=Q15932564|lt=List of Shinto shrines in the United States|1=List of Shinto shrines in the United States}} ==External links== * [https://www.facebook.com/pages/Izumo-Taishakyo-Mission-of-Hawaii/162743883736181 Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii] on Facebook ==References== {{reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{Izumo-taishakyo}} {{Source Citation|date=June 7, 2015 (Sun) 09:48 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| |name = Fuwai Izumo Taisha
Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} |image = [[File:IZUMO TAISHA-KYO MISSION 01.jpg|300px]] |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} |location = 215 North Kukui Street Honolulu HI 96817-3951 |coordinates = {{ill|Wiki Coordinates (2-level DMS)|ja|ウィキ座標2段度分秒|21|18|55.9|N|157|51|39.6|W|region:US_type:landmark|display=inline,title|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wiki Coordinates (2-level DMS)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |map = {{ill|Map|en|Map|qid=Q4006|lt=Location map|1=Map}} |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|lt=Great Okuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}, Hawaii {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|qid=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}} |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} |type = |founded = {{ill|1906|en|1906|qid=Q2049|lt=1906|1=1906}} |main_hall = {{ill|Taisha-zukuri|en|Taisha-zukuri|qid=Q387874|lt=Taisha-zukuri|1=Taisha-zukuri}} |alias = Izumo Taisha Hawaii Branch |shrines = |religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} |festival = First Sunday of early October |rituals = }} '''Hawaii Izumo Taisha''' (Hawaii Izumo Taisha, Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|United States|en|United States|qid=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}}, {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|qid=Q782|lt=Hawaii|1=Hawaii}}, {{ill|Honolulu|ja|ホノルル市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honolulu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホノルル|1=Honolulu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18094}}. {{ill|1885|en|1885|qid=Q7821|lt=1885|1=1885}} (18th year of Meiji) under the Japan–Hawaii Immigration Treaty was enshrined in {{ill|1906|en|1906|qid=Q2049|lt=1906|1=1906}} (39th year of Meiji) by {{ill|Japanese in Hawaii|en|Japanese in Hawaii|qid=Q6158764|lt=Japanese immigrants who migrated to Hawaii|1=Japanese in Hawaii}}. It is also known as '''Izumo Taisha Hawaii Branch''' as a branch of {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|qid=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}} in Shimane Prefecture[http://promotion.yahoo.co.jp/news/anas/1607 Goshuin Girl, Crossing the Sea — In Hawaii "Izumo Taisha", a gradual boom in collecting overseas goshuin]. The {{ill|Principal Deity|ja|主祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Principal Deity|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭神|1=Saijin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591100}} is {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|lt=Great Okuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}, Hawaii {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|qid=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}}. == History and Tradition == * {{ill|1906|en|1906|qid=Q2049|lt=1906|1=1906}} – A [[:en:Kannushi|Shinto Priest]] from {{ill|Hiroshima Prefecture|en|Hiroshima Prefecture|qid=Q617375|lt=Hiroshima Prefecture|1=Hiroshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Miyao Katsuyoshi|ja|宮王勝良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyao Katsuyoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579348|1=Q135579348}}, was dispatched to Hawaii on the order of the 2nd head of {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|qid=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Religion|1=Izumo-taishakyo}}, {{ill|Sengetakaaki|ja|千家尊愛|lt=Sengetakaaki|lt_ja=せんげたかあき|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengetakaaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579349|1=Sengetakaaki}}, and began propagating the Izumo faith in the Ala Moana area of Honolulu. * {{ill|1907|en|1907|qid=Q2048|lt=1907|1=1907}} – In the downtown area at the triangular intersection of King Street and Beretania Street (Parama area), the precincts were established and the first church hall and shrine were completed. * {{ill|1909|en|1909|qid=Q2057|lt=1909|1=1909}} – Officially recognized by {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|qid=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Religion|1=Izumo-taishakyo}} as the "Izumo Taisha Religion Fuwai Church Hall". Church halls and {{ill|Kosha|ja|講社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 講|1=Kō (lecture)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11632184}} were established on {{ill|Maui|en|Maui|qid=Q188705|lt=Maui Island|1=Maui}}, [[:en:Hawaii (island)|Hawaii Island]], and other areas, and active operations were carried out. * {{ill|1918|en|1918|qid=Q2094|lt=1918|1=1918}} – Elevated to the "Izumo Taisha Religion Fuwai Branch" with {{ill|Miyao Katsuyoshi|ja|宮王勝良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyao Katsuyoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579348|1=Q135579348}} as the first branch head. The following year, the legal organization of the Hawaii Izumo Taisha religious group was recognized by the {{ill|Territory of Hawaii|ja|ハワイ準州|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Territory of Hawaii|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハワイ州|1=Hawaii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q782}} government. * {{ill|1922|en|1922|qid=Q2165|lt=1922|1=1922}} – A new shrine building, modeled after the main shrine, was constructed on the premises. The following year, a grand festival was held to welcome the delegation of the 3rd head of {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|qid=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Religion|1=Izumo-taishakyo}}, {{ill|Sengetakamochi|ja|千家尊有|lt=Sengetakamochi|lt_ja=せんげたかもち|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengetakamochi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579350|1=Sengetakamochi}}, and others. * {{ill|1935|en|1935|qid=Q18658|lt=1935|1=1935}} – {{ill|Miyao Katsuyoshi|ja|宮王勝良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyao Katsuyoshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579348|1=Q135579348}} returned to Japan. After obtaining Shinto priest qualifications in the Empire of Japan, his second son, {{ill|Miyao Shigemaru|ja|宮王重丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyao Shigemaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579351|1=Q135579351}}, who had been serving in Hawaii since 1931, assumed the position of the 2nd branch head. * {{ill|1941|en|1941|qid=Q5231|lt=1941|1=1941}} – Due to the {{ill|Attack on Pearl Harbor|en|Attack on Pearl Harbor|qid=Q52418|lt=Attack on Pearl Harbor|1=Attack on Pearl Harbor}}, {{ill|Miyao Shigemaru|ja|宮王重丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyao Shigemaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579351|1=Q135579351}}, his family, and branch officials were detained by the {{ill|Federal Bureau of Investigation|en|Federal Bureau of Investigation|qid=Q8333|lt=FBI|1=Federal Bureau of Investigation}}, and were held on the U.S. mainland until the end of the war in 1945. During their detention, the religious group disbanded, and the shrine and other properties were donated to the Honolulu City and County government, being managed by the Parks and Sanitation Departments. * {{ill|1946|en|1946|qid=Q18610|lt=1946|1=1946}} – At the end of the previous year, upon his return from detention, {{ill|Miyao Shigemaru|ja|宮王重丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyao Shigemaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579351|1=Q135579351}} renovated a warehouse on Young Street into a shrine building, and resumed activities as a shrine from the New Year. * {{ill|1952|en|1952|qid=Q5272|lt=1952|1=1952}} – The Hawaii Izumo Taisha religious group, with {{ill|Miyao Shigemaru|ja|宮王重丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyao Shigemaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579351|1=Q135579351}} as the plaintiff, gathered over 10,000 signatures and submitted a petition to the Honolulu City Council seeking the return of the shrine building. Although the return was unanimously resolved, the Parks Department contested the return, resulting in prolonged disputes. * {{ill|1961|en|1961|qid=Q3696|lt=1961|1=1961}} – After six rounds of movements for return, the religious group ultimately prevailed in court, and the shrine building was returned by the Honolulu City and County government. * {{ill|1968|en|1968|qid=Q2644|lt=1968|1=1968}} – Following the redevelopment plan for Kukui Street, the shrine building was relocated and restored to its current location, and a relocation festival was held. The following year, under the service of the 5th head of {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|qid=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Religion|1=Izumo-taishakyo}}, {{ill|Sengemichihiko|ja|千家達彦|lt=Sengemichihiko|lt_ja=せんげみちひこ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengemichihiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579352|1=Sengemichihiko}}, the "Fuwai Izumo Taisha Completion Celebration and Combined Annual Festival" was observed. * {{ill|1976|en|1976|qid=Q2480|lt=1976|1=1976}} – In commemoration of the 200th anniversary of the founding of the United States, during a tour in Hawaii, sumo wrestlers of the {{ill|Japan Sumo Association|en|Japan Sumo Association|qid=Q1426308|lt=Japan Sumo Association|1=Japan Sumo Association}} performed a {{ill|Yokozuna|ja|横綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokozuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Yokozuna|qid=Q1067277}} {{ill|dohyō-iri|ja|土俵入り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/dohyō-iri|1=dohyō-iri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11423077}} ceremony as an offering. * {{ill|1990|en|1990|qid=Q2064|lt=1990|1=1990}} – Beside the {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|qid=Q234731|lt=Torii|1=Torii}} at the front of the shrine, a "Hiroshima Peace Bell" commemorating the sister city relationship between Honolulu and Hiroshima (established in 1960) was installed by the Honolulu City and County government. * {{ill|1993|en|1993|qid=Q2065|lt=1993|1=1993}} – {{ill|Miyao Shigemaru|ja|宮王重丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyao Shigemaru|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579351|1=Q135579351}} returned to Japan. The current leader, Mr. Daiya Amano (天野大也), assumed the position of the 3rd branch head. * {{ill|2006|en|2006|qid=Q2021|lt=2006|1=2006}} – Under the service of the 5th head of {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|qid=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Religion|1=Izumo-taishakyo}}, {{ill|Sengemichihiko|ja|千家達彦|lt=Sengemichihiko|lt_ja=せんげみちひこ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengemichihiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579352|1=Sengemichihiko}}, the "Fuwai Izumo Taisha 100th Anniversary Grand Festival" was held{{sfn|前田孝和|1999|p=149-182}}{{sfn|Miyao, Richard T., ed.|2006|pp=76-93}}. == Others == * The shrine office reception hours are from 8:30 AM to 5:00 PM. Due to the closure of the front gate outside these hours, visits for {{ill|worship|ja|参拝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/worship|1=Sanpai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410186}} are not allowed. The area surrounding the shrine is one of the less secure areas even within Honolulu, and visiting after dark is extremely dangerous{{sfn|Hawaii Pacific Press|p=7}}. Prayers and related activities require prior reservation. * In addition to the principal deity, as guardian deities for immigrants from Okinawa Prefecture, branch spirits from {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|en|Okinawa Shrine|qid=Q11139392|lt=Okinawa Shrine|1=Okinawa Shrine}}, {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Hagami Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, and {{ill|Futemmangu Shrine|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemmangu Shrine|1=Futemma-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11514556}} were enshrined in 1941. Branch deities from {{ill|Ebisu Shrine|ja|恵比寿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to えびす神社}}, {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|qid=Q514480|lt=Inari Shrine|1=Inari shrine}}, the guardian deity of the Wai'anae area (which was the Kosha of Hawaii Izumo Taisha), and ancestral shrines enshrined throughout [[:en:Oahu|Oʻahu]] were also enshrined{{sfn|IZUMO TAISHA NEWSLETTER|Spring 2012|p=4}}. * During the New Year, on the {{ill|Three Days|ja|三ヶ日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Days|1=Three Days|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11354062}} period for {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|qid=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumode|1=Hatsumōde}}, over 10,000 worshipers, primarily local residents of Hawaii, visit, and not only those of Japanese ancestry but people of various ethnic backgrounds are in attendance{{sfn|Miyao, Richard T., ed.|2006|pp=102-105}}. * Every year on {{ill|August 6|en|August 6|qid=Q2787|lt=August 6|1=August 6}}, at the "Hiroshima Peace Bell," prayers for world peace are offered in conjunction with religious figures from other religions such as {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|qid=Q5043|lt=Christianity|1=Christianity}}, and {{ill|Judaism|en|Judaism|qid=Q9268|lt=Judaism|1=Judaism}}.{{sfn|Miyao, Richard T., ed.|2006|pp=111-113}} == Transportation Access == * Approximately 25 minutes by {{ill|TheBus (Honolulu)|en|TheBus (Honolulu)|qid=Q7711627|lt=TheBus|1=TheBus (Honolulu)}} from Waikiki. '''On routes 2 and 13, get off at the Hotel & River stop, and then walk approximately 5 minutes. '''On routes 19, 20, 42, and B, get off at the Beretania & River stop, and then walk approximately 5 minutes. * Approximately 20 minutes by {{ill|TheBus (Honolulu)|en|TheBus (Honolulu)|qid=Q7711627|lt=TheBus|1=TheBus (Honolulu)}} from Ala Moana Shopping Center. '''On routes 19, 20, 42, 52, 53, and 62, get off at the Beretania & River stop, and then walk approximately 5 minutes. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Bibliography == {{Refbegin|2}} * {{cite book|language=Japanese|author=前田孝和|title=ハワイの神社史|publisher=大明堂|date=1999|isbn=4470200476|ref=harv}} * {{cite book|language=Japanese|author=出雲大社社務所|title=出雲大社由緒略記|date=1984|location=大社町 (島根県)|publisher=出雲大社社務所|ref=harv}} * {{Cite journal|journal=IZUMO TAISHA NEWSLETTER|date=Spring 2012|publisher=Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii|ref={{SfnRef|IZUMO TAISHA NEWSLETTER|2012}}}} * {{Cite journal|journal=HAWAII PACIFIC PRESS|date=March 1, 2014|publisher=Hawaii Pacific Press|ref={{SfnRef|Hawaii Pacific Press|2014}}}} * {{Cite book|author=Miyao, Richard T., ed.|title=Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii Centennial Anniversary: Saga of a Church in Hawaii 1906-2006|location=Honolulu, USA|publisher=Izumo Taishakyo Mission of Hawaii|date=2006|oclc=663406371|ref=harv}} {{Refend}} == External Links == * [http://izumo-taishakyo-hawaii.com/ Hawaii Izumo Taisha] * {{facebook|Izumo-Taishakyo-Mission-of-Hawaii-162743883736181}} * [http://himoji.jp/database/db04/preview.php?name=%E3%83%8F%E3%83%AF%E3%82%A4%E5%87%BA%E9%9B%B2%E5%A4%A7%E7%A4%BE Hawaii Izumo Taisha (Izumo Taisha Religion Hawaii Branch)] – Database on overseas shrines (former site) by the Non-textual Materials Research Center of {{ill|Kanagawa University|en|Kanagawa University|qid=Q203624|lt=Kanagawa University|1=Kanagawa University}}. {{Shinto-stub}}
